title: The Outlaw’s Oath
blurb: The past isn’t buried. It’s waiting in the shadows of a Buenos Aires club where deals are made—and broken. A surgeon who was once a ghost must confront the boy he failed before the truth tears the city apart. One ledger. Two deadlines. One shot at redemption—or ruin.
comparable: For fans of *The President’s Hat* by Antoine Laurain, *The Ministry of Utmost Happiness* by Arundhati Roy, and *The Shadow of the Wind* by Carlos Ruiz Zafón.

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The stainless steel OR light gleamed against the late morning haze filtering through the high windows of Valente & Asociados. Rigo adjusted the scope, felt the familiar weight of the catheter in his hand. No tremor. No hesitation. The patient on the table—a sixty-three-year-old banker with a 97% occlusion in the LAD—wasn’t even aware of the storm inside him.

Nurse López held the drape steady. “Such steady hands, Doctor. You could almost be making coffee instead of cracking hearts open.”

Rigo didn’t look up. “Coffee would’ve been easier.”

Intern Gutierrez hovered near the monitor, staring at the EKG as if willing the ST segment to behave. “You ever miss it? The real cases?”

Cecilia stood beside the anaesthesiologist, arms crossed, watching Rigo like a specimen under glass. “He still gets them. Just not the ones that bleed.”

The words landed like a dropped scalpel. Rigo didn’t flinch. He never did.

The cath lab was quiet except for the rhythmic beep of the monitor and the soft hiss of the ventilator. He threaded the catheter through the aorta, felt the familiar resistance as it breached the blockage. The patient’s vitals stayed flat, textbook. Perfect.

López passed him the stent. He slipped it into place with the ease of long practice. No drama. No fanfare. Just precision.

Inside the control booth, Gutierrez exhaled. “That’s why we train under him. No wasted motion. No panic.”

Cecilia uncrossed her arms. “Panic’s only wasted if you don’t know what you’re afraid of.”

The words hung between them. Rigo didn’t react. He never did.

---

The procedure ended in under forty minutes. The patient woke smoothly, groggy but alive. Rigo gave the post-op orders without looking at Cecilia, handed the chart to López.

“Keep him monitored. No surprises.”

“Of course, Doctor,” López said, scribbling notes. “You ever have surprises?”

Rigo walked past her, paused at the sink. Washed his hands with surgical soap, scrubbing until the water ran clear. “Only the ones I make.”

---

He stepped into his office—a converted storage closet with a window that looked onto a brick wall and a framed medical degree that had nothing to do with the man who’d earned it. The clinic was quiet after lunch. Most of the staff had dispersed for café cortados or siestas.

Rigo hung his lab coat on the back of the chair. Sat. Opened the leather portfolio on his desk. Inside were patient charts, insurance forms, a half-finished research paper on residual scarring from bypass grafts. None of it important.

His fingers brushed the gold-trimmed Borges collection on the shelf behind him. The microfilm wasn’t there. He knew exactly where it was: in the safety deposit box beneath the Bank of the Argentine Republic on Corrientes. The ledger in the vault, the reel in the book—both untouched since the day he’d buried his old life.

A knock at the door.

Cecilia stood there, one hand on the frame, the other holding a manila folder. She wore a white coat over a silk blouse the color of ripe cherries. It made her lips look darker.

“You’re avoiding me,” she said.

Rigo didn’t answer. He didn’t have to.

She stepped inside, shut the door behind her. The click of the latch was too loud.

“I scheduled your psych eval for next Tuesday.”

He looked up. “You didn’t.”

“I did.” She placed the folder on his desk. It landed with a dull thud. “Hospital policy. After three incidents in two weeks.”

Incidents. A patient’s relative shouting about malpractice. A nurse overheard him in the hallway muttering to a wall. A junior resident catching him staring at a framed photo in the break room—black and white, no one he recognized.

Rigo opened the folder. Inside was a referral form and a checklist of symptoms: agitation, insomnia, intrusive memories. The handwriting was clinical. Impersonal.

He flipped the top page. The second was blank.

Except for one line, scrawled in red ink across the bottom:

*They know where Mateo is.*

The ink was still wet. A single drop smudged on the edge of the paper like a tear.

Rigo’s breath didn’t change. His pulse didn’t race. He folded the folder closed, set it precisely in the center of his desk.

Cecilia watched him, eyes narrowing. “That’s not hospital policy.”

He met her gaze. Held it. “No. It isn’t.”

Silence stretched. Outside, a delivery van rattled past on Gorriti. Somewhere down the hall, Gutierrez laughed.

Cecilia’s fingers tightened on the doorknob. “Tell me what’s happening, Rigo.”

He didn’t answer. Couldn’t.

The folder stayed where it was.

Chapter 2

El Tobogán glowed.

Twelve-foot ceilings arched over white linen and damask. Waiters in tailcoats moved through the crowd carrying silver trays of champagne flutes. The scent of cigar smoke and gardenia perfume layered thick over the polished hardwood. A string quartet played a tango in the corner—too polite, too rehearsed, the kind of music that made old men tap their feet while younger ones checked their watches.

Rigo stood at the edge of the ballroom, a glass of Malbec in hand he had no intention of drinking. His black suit was the same cut as the one he’d buried fifteen years ago, just newer. The temperature at the back of his neck was exactly right—neither too hot nor too cold, like a surgical drape warmed under a lamp.

Javier Albarracín stood on the dais beneath the chandeliers, adjusting his reading glasses before lifting a speech from the lectern. The man had always had the posture of a man who believed gravity favored him.

“Ladies and gentlemen,” Javier began, his voice calibrated for theatrical resonance, “we gather tonight not to celebrate the past, but to protect the future.”

A ripple of polite applause.

“Recent events suggest that certain individuals,”—a pause, deliberate, surgical—“are operating outside the law with impunity. Clinics that masquerade as healers. Professionals who wear the cloak of medicine while wielding the daggers of impunity.”

Cecilia appeared beside Rigo like a reflection in the wrong mirror—her cherry blouse now under a dark shawl, her dark hair swept into something formal, something that didn’t belong here. She held a clutch purse, tight in both hands.

“He’s not naming names,” she murmured. “Yet.”

Rigo didn’t turn. “He doesn’t need to.”

“Valente & Asociados,” Javier continued, “has been cited in three separate complaints to the Medical Board. Unverified, anonymous, but persistent. And in a city that prides itself on transparency, we must ask: what are they hiding?”

The room held its breath. A judge from Mar del Plata coughed into a napkin. A prosecutor from San Isidro shifted in his chair.

Then—

“And then there’s the matter of memory,” Javier said, leaning slightly forward. “In this city, we’ve learned that the past has teeth. That it doesn’t always stay buried. That some ghosts refuse to lie down.”

A murmur rolled through the crowd. Someone dropped a glass. It shattered on the parquet with a sound like gunfire.

Rigo set his untouched wine on a passing tray. His fingers didn’t tremble. They rarely did.

Cecilia’s knuckles went white around her purse. “He knows.”

“He suspects,” Rigo corrected.

“Suspects enough to say it here. In front of them.”

Rigo tracked the room. Judges. Prosecutors. University chairs. The men who signed the checks that kept the Medical Board in line. All watching Javier with renewed deference, as if the Supreme Court justice had just performed a miracle.

Then his gaze landed on the far side of the ballroom.

Mateo Ibarra.

The journalist stood beside a potted ficus, holding a glass of soda, watching Rigo with the quiet intensity of a man who had spent a decade dissecting corpses of the past. He wore a gray suit, no tie, the collar unbuttoned. The boy Rigo had failed to protect was now a man who had made it his mission to expose him.

Mateo took a sip. Smiled.

It wasn’t a friendly smile.

Cecilia followed Rigo’s stare. “Who is that?”

“No one.”

“He’s looking at you like he wants to cut you open.”

Rigo didn’t answer.

Javier finished his speech with a flourish. The room erupted in applause. Waiters rushed forward with fresh trays. The chandeliers flickered, as if the power grid needed a reminder of who owned the light.

Rigo turned toward the hallway leading to the restrooms and private chambers. He needed air. Space. A moment to think.

Cecilia caught his arm. Her grip was firm, not gentle. “You’re leaving?”

“I’m avoiding a scene.”

“That ship sailed when he said your clinic’s name.”

Rigo pulled free. Walked down the hallway carpeted in deep burgundy. The walls were lined with oil paintings of dead presidents and generals. No one watched him go.

The door to the men’s room was oak, heavy. He stepped inside. The scent of cedar and old money filled his lungs. He washed his hands at the marble sink. Soap in a silver dispenser, chilled to the touch. He dried them on a linen towel provided by the club.

The door opened behind him.

He didn’t turn.

“I saw you talking to him,” Javier said, voice smooth, practiced, like a scalpel drawn across skin. “Valente. Outside the clinic last week. Early morning. You looked like two men who shared a grave.”

Rigo folded the towel and placed it on the rack. “And what did you do with that information?”

“I filed it. Just in case.”

“In case of what?”

“In case you decided to resurrect your ghost.”

Rigo finally turned. Javier stood in the doorway, his suit impeccable, his expression unreadable behind the sheen of public virtue. The Supreme Court justice was a man who had spent decades polishing his reputation like a mirror, making sure every reflection showed only what he wanted seen.

“You’re performing surgery on living men now,” Javier said. “But I know what you did to the dead.”

Rigo stepped closer. The air between them compressed.

“You built a career on war crimes,” Rigo said quietly. “I made them disappear. Tell me, Javier—who’s the more dangerous man?”

Javier’s smile didn’t waver. “One of us took an oath. The other took a bribe.”

The door clicked shut behind Javier as he left.

Rigo stood alone in the bathroom. The silence pressed in. He flexed his fingers. They moved like they had in the OR—precise, controlled.

Then he walked out.

The hallway was empty. The gala buzzed on like a hive behind closed doors. He reached the coat check. His overcoat hung on a brass hook. The attendant, a young man with slicked hair and a too-tight uniform, barely glanced up as Rigo took it.

He stepped into the elevator. The doors slid shut. The descent was smooth, quiet. He pressed the button for the ground floor.

Halfway down, the elevator stopped.

The lights flickered.

A single voice came over the intercom, distorted, unfamiliar.

“Doctor Valente. We need to talk.”

The doors didn’t open.

Rigo stood perfectly still. His pulse didn’t rise. His breath didn’t quicken. But his right hand slipped into his coat pocket and closed around the scalpel he carried—clean, sterile, unused.

He had not brought a weapon to a war.

But he had always kept one in his pocket.

The intercom crackled again.

“Security has been notified.”

The elevator lurched. The lights steadied. The doors slid open on the lobby.

Javier Albarracín stood there, flanked by two men in dark suits. His expression was calm. Almost sympathetic.

“You’re coming with us,” Javier said.

Rigo stepped out. The lobby was quiet. The string quartet’s tango had segued into something slower, sadder.

Cecilia stood near the entrance, her shawl draped over her shoulders, her face pale in the golden light. She mouthed a single word:

*Why?*

Rigo didn’t answer.

He walked past them all.

The night air hit him like a slap. The city pulsed—horns, shouts, the distant growl of a bus. He pulled out his car keys. The Mercedes started on the first try.

As he pulled away from the curb, he checked the rearview.

Javier was still standing there. Watching.

Then Mateo stepped into the frame. He lit a cigarette. Exhaled smoke into the night.

Rigo drove.

The Mercedes merged into traffic on Corrientes. Ahead, the Obelisk stood like a needle stitching the sky.

His hands gripped the wheel.

No panic.

No fear.

Just calculation.

He reached into the glove box. Pulled out a manila envelope. Inside was a key and an address.

The safety deposit box beneath the Bank of the Argentine Republic on Corrientes.

The ledger was still there.

The microfilm reel still hidden in the Borges.

And the coded names still held half the judicial establishment hostage.

Mateo thought he was hunting a ghost.

But ghosts don’t keep ledgers.

Ghosts don’t plan revenge.

Ghosts don’t sign their real names to blackmail.

Rigo turned the wheel hard. The car surged forward. He needed to get to the vault before dawn.

Before Cecilia’s psychiatric evaluation became a reality.

Before Mateo’s article went to print.

Before Javier’s confirmation vote locked the Supreme Court into place forever.

The radio played a milonga. A voice sang about lost love and broken promises.

Rigo reached over and turned it off.

He had a city to burn.

Chapter 3

Valente & Asociados smelled like antiseptic and flaking bureaucracy.

Rigo locked the clinic door at 7:14 p.m., the deadbolt clicking with finality. The waiting room was empty except for a stack of medical journals fanned across the reception desk. He didn’t look at them.

His office was a converted exam room with a view of a brick wall. He dropped his coat on the chair and sat at the steel desk. The chair wheezed under his weight.

Cecilia’s voice came from the phone on his desk:

*Rodrigo, please call me back. The psychiatric evaluation is scheduled for tomorrow at nine. I’ve cleared your schedule. Don’t make this harder than it has to be.*

He tapped the screen. The message deleted.

The office hummed with the sound of an ancient radiator struggling against the autumn chill. Rigo opened the top drawer. Inside rested a ledger wrapped in oilcloth, tied with twine. He untied it. The pages smelled like mildew and old paper.

He lit a match.

One corner of the first page curled black. Flame licked upward, slow and deliberate. He held it over the metal wastebasket until the edge caught. Orange embers drifted up like lost fireflies.

The ledger shrank. Names he had written in code fifty years ago turned to ash. He fed it sheet by sheet. The smoke curled toward the ceiling, carrying secrets with it.

His hands didn’t shake.

The phone buzzed again. Cecilia. He ignored it.

He reached for the second drawer. Beneath prescription pads and tongue depressors lay a stainless steel surgical scalpel wrapped in gauze. He stripped away the wrapping. The blade gleamed under the fluorescent light.

He tested the edge with his thumb. A thin red line appeared.

He slipped the scalpel into his coat pocket. The metal weighed nothing, but it felt like a promise.

The intercom on the wall crackled.

“Dr. Valente?” The receptionist’s voice, hesitant. “You have a visitor.”

“Not receiving.”

“Says his name is Mateo Ibarra.”

Rigo exhaled through his nose. He didn’t touch the intercom button.

“Tell him to leave.”

“I did. He won’t.”

A pause. Then footsteps in the hall. The clinic door opened.

Mateo stood framed in the doorway, taller than Rigo remembered, his shoulders broader. He wore a black leather jacket over a faded Rolling Stones shirt. His hair was longer, tied back. Fifteen years had carved new lines around his eyes.

He didn’t smile.

“You burned the ledger,” Mateo said. “I saw the smoke.”

Rigo didn’t stand. “Then you saw nothing.”

Mateo stepped inside. The door swung shut behind him. The clinic’s clock ticked loud, as if measuring the time left before something broke.

“You don’t get to walk away,” Mateo said. “Not this time.”

“Walk away from what? I’m a retired surgeon. You’re a journalist. We have nothing to say to each other.”

Mateo moved to the window. He pressed his palm against the glass. “You always had a flair for the dramatic. Even in medical school. Remember that time in anatomy lab when you cut the femoral artery by accident and nearly bled out the cadaver?”

Rigo didn’t answer.

“They told me you were a ghost,” Mateo said. “That you disappeared after the operation. That you left without a word. That you let everything collapse.”

“You were a boy. Boys don’t understand operations.”

“You were supposed to protect me.”

Rigo’s fingers twitched on the desk. He kept them still.

“I protected you the only way I could,” Rigo said. “I erased your name. Your face. Your existence. I made sure no file ever linked you to the operation. You walked out of that hospital a ghost. No record. No danger. That was the best I could do.”

“By turning me into a ghost? That’s not protection.”

“It was survival. For both of us.”

Mateo turned. His eyes were dark, unreadable. “Then survive this. Stop burning evidence. Stop pretending you’re just a doctor.”

Rigo leaned back in the chair. The springs groaned.

“What do you want, Mateo?”

“I want the truth. All of it. The names. The dates. The ones who gave the orders. I want to know who signed the death warrants. Not in code. Not in whispers. In print. For the world to see.”

“And then?”

“Then we see if Argentina is ready to face what it did.”

Rigo stood. He walked to the window. The brick wall outside was clean, unadorned. No graffiti. No cracks.

“You think publishing will change anything? You think the judges will resign in shame? The politicians will weep? This isn’t a morality play. This is a transaction. Power doesn’t repent. It buries the dead deeper.”

“Then I’ll dig.”

Rigo turned. “You’re playing a game you don’t understand. The stakes aren’t articles. They’re lives. Not just yours. Mine. Cecilia’s. Inés’s.”

Mateo stiffened at the name. His jaw tightened.

“Inés,” he repeated softly. “You still talk to her?”

“Not in years.”

“She thinks you’re a hero.”

Rigo almost laughed. “She thinks I’m a man who saved her brother. That’s not the same thing.”

“It could be. If you let it.”

Rigo moved past him, toward the door. “This conversation is over.”

Mateo blocked the exit. He was taller, but Rigo had carried bodies heavier than this man in the dark.

“You’re going to run again,” Mateo said. “Like you ran fifteen years ago. But I’m not a boy anymore. I know where you live. I know where you keep your secrets. And I know exactly where you’ll go next.”

Rigo didn’t push him. He didn’t raise his voice. He simply said, “Then you know more than you should.”

Mateo stepped aside. Just enough. Rigo reached for the door.

“I have the next article ready,” Mateo said to his back. “It goes live in two days. Your name is in it. Your clinic. Your past. Everything.”

Rigo paused. The air between them thickened.

“You wouldn’t.”

“I would. Unless you give me something real. Something that matters. Not ashes. Not ledgers. Something that can’t be burned.”

Rigo opened the door. The hallway was empty. Fluorescent lights buzzed overhead.

He didn’t look back. “Good luck with your article, Mateo.”

The door clicked shut behind him.

Rigo walked down the dim corridor. The linoleum was scuffed from years of shoes moving too fast or too slow. He reached the coat rack. His jacket hung there like a second skin.

He slipped it on. The scalpel in his pocket pressed against his thigh.

Outside, the city breathed—engines, shouts, the distant wail of a police siren. He pulled out his car keys. The Mercedes was parked half a block away, safe under a streetlamp.

He had one stop left before dawn.

One more ledger page to burn.

One more name to erase.

The phone in his pocket buzzed again. Cecilia. He silenced it.

He got into the car. The engine turned over on the first try.

As he pulled away, he caught a glimpse of himself in the rearview—the face of a retired surgeon in a worn suit, driving home through a city that had forgotten him.

But the scalpel in his pocket remembered everything.

And in three hours, he would teach it how to forget again.

Chapter 4

The estancia’s library smelled of leather and cigar smoke. Javier Albarracín held a flute of champagne in one hand, a leather-bound book in the other. He stood by the fireplace, the flames casting gold flecks across his cufflinks. Around him, the men laughed, their voices low, polished. The women sipped wine and murmured about the confirmation vote.

“To justice,” Javier said, lifting his glass. “And to the future.”

The others echoed him. Laura, his wife, stood at his elbow, her smile steady, her eyes scanning the room. She wore a dark green dress that matched the Tuscany landscape on the wall behind her. She didn’t drink.

“You’ll be magnificent on the bench,” Senator Albarracín said, clapping Javier on the shoulder. “The youngest Supreme Court justice in decades. History will remember this.”

Javier smiled. “History is written by the victors.”

Valeria Mendez, the journalist, tilted her head. “And you’re positioning yourself to be one of them.”

“Positioning?” Javier laughed. “I’m not positioning. I’m confirming.”

Laura’s fingers tightened around her glass. She glanced at her husband, then away.

A butler appeared in the doorway. “Señor Justice. Dinner is served.”

Javier nodded. “Thank you, Enrique. We’ll be down shortly.”

The butler retreated. The group began to move toward the dining room, their voices rising in anticipation. Laura lingered, her heel tapping against the Persian rug.

“You’re distracted,” Javier said quietly.

She didn’t look at him. “The clinic files. They’re missing.”

Javier waved a hand. “A clerical error. Someone misplaced a box.”

“It was Valente’s clinic. Rodrigo Valente.”

Javier’s smile didn’t waver. “And?”

“And you accused him of crimes. Now his files are gone. Doesn’t that seem convenient?”

Javier set his glass on the mantel. “Convenient for whom? If he’s guilty, the files would incriminate him. If he’s innocent, their disappearance is irrelevant. Either way, it changes nothing.”

Laura finally looked at him. “You’re dismissing it too quickly.”

“Because it is too quickly dismissed.” He stepped closer. “The confirmation vote is in twenty-eight days. The Senate is poised to confirm me. The past is buried, Laura. It has to stay buried.”

She studied him, her dark eyes sharp. “You’re not as confident as you pretend.”

Javier exhaled through his nose. “I’m confident enough to know when to worry and when to move on. The surgeon is retired. The ledger is ash. The boy—”

“Mateo Ibarra.”

“—is a journalist with a deadline. Once his article runs, we’ll see if anyone cares.”

Laura’s lips thinned. “You underestimate him.”

“No. I underestimate the public’s patience.” He turned toward the hallway. “Now, let’s eat. I’m hungry.”

She didn’t follow. “Javier.”

He paused.

“What if it’s not about the past?” she asked. “What if it’s about now?”

“Now is about power. And power is something I know how to hold.”

She opened her mouth to argue, but he was already walking away, his shoes clicking against the wooden floor. The others were waiting. The conversation was over.

---

Downstairs, the dining room was set for eight. Candles flickered in silver holders. The scent of roasted lamb and garlic filled the air. Javier took his seat at the head of the table, his posture perfect, his smile effortless. The others settled into their chairs, their voices rising like a well-rehearsed quartet.

Laura sat to his left, her shoulders tense. Valeria Mendez was across from her, pen and notebook in hand. “Senator Albarracín tells me you’ve already begun planning your first cases,” she said.

Javier speared a piece of lamb with his fork. “I have. The judicial system is a machine. It only needs the right hands to turn the crank.”

“And the right narrative,” Valeria added.

“Exactly.” He chewed slowly, savoring the meat. “Justice isn’t about morality. It’s about order. And order is what this country needs.”

Senator Albarracín raised his glass. “To order.”

The others drank. Laura didn’t.

Javier cut into the lamb again. “Tell me, Valeria, how goes your investigation into the recent murders? Any leads?”

Valeria set down her fork. “I have a source. Claims the killings are connected to old operations. Black ops from the dictatorship.”

Javier’s knife paused mid-air. “A bold claim.”

“One that implicates powerful men.”

He met her gaze, unblinking. “And women.”

She didn’t flinch. “Yes. Women too.”

Laura’s fingers drummed against the tablecloth. Javier pretended not to notice.

“Careful, Valeria,” he said lightly. “You don’t want to be accused of defamation. Or worse.”

Valeria smiled. “I’m careful with facts, not accusations.”

Javier set his fork down. “Facts have consequences. You know that better than most.”

The table fell silent. The candles burned low.

Laura cleared her throat. “Javier, the butler mentioned something else. A message from Dr. Rau.”

Javier’s jaw tightened. “Cecilia.”

“She said it was urgent. Something about an audit.”

Javier exhaled through his nose. “Cecilia means well, but she’s paranoid. She thinks every irregularity is a scandal.”

“She’s the Chief of Cardiology,” Laura said. “Audits are her purview.”

“And I’m the justice overseeing the judiciary.” He picked up his napkin and dabbed his lips. “Tell her I’ll call her tomorrow.”

The silence stretched. Valeria’s pen scratched against her notebook.

Then, from the hallway, Enrique’s voice cut through the tension:

“Señor Justice. Forgive the interruption, but there’s a matter in your study that requires your attention.”

Javier’s fork clattered against his plate. “Now?”

Enrique stood in the doorway, his face unreadable. “I’m afraid so.”

Javier pushed back his chair. “Excuse me.”

He walked out without another word, his shoes echoing against the tile. The others watched him go, their expressions a mix of curiosity and concern.

Laura rose. “I’ll come with you.”

Javier didn’t look back. “Stay. Enjoy your dinner.”

She didn’t sit.

---

The study was at the end of the hall, its door slightly ajar. Javier pushed it open.

The room was dark. The desk lamp was off. He flipped the switch.

The light revealed a scene of controlled chaos. Papers were strewn across the desk, drawers hung open, the floor littered with file folders. His breath caught.

Enrique stood in the doorway, his hands clasped behind his back. “I found it like this, señor. No sign of forced entry.”

Javier moved to the desk. His fingers brushed a file labeled *Valente & Asociados – Compliance*. He flipped it open. Inside were clinic records, patient files, financial statements—nothing incriminating. Just paperwork.

He tossed it aside.

His eye caught a ledger on the floor, half-hidden under a chair. He knelt and picked it up. The cover was leather, the pages yellowed. He thumbed through it. Names, dates, codes. His pulse spiked.

Then he saw it.

Page 173.

His name.

Page 173.

A subheading: *Operation Clean Hands – Primary Investigator.*

His stomach dropped.

Enrique cleared his throat. “Señor?”

Javier slammed the ledger shut. “Find out who did this.”

“Of course, señor.”

Javier’s hands trembled. He stuffed the ledger into his coat pocket.

Outside, the laughter from the dining room drifted down the hall, warm and oblivious.

He took a deep breath.

Then he walked back to the door.

But the ledger was burning a hole in his pocket.

Chapter 5

The Ministry of Health building on Avenida 9 de Julio was a slab of concrete and glass, all right angles and fluorescent glare. Javier Albarracín stepped out of his Mercedes, his polished shoes clicking against the pavement. Inspector Rivas fell into step beside him, his suit jacket straining over a gut that had seen too much empanada and not enough stairs.

“You’re certain Valente’s clinic is the weak link,” Rivas said.

Javier adjusted his cuffs. “A man who retires to a quiet practice is either prudent or hiding. Rigo Valente is neither.”

They took the elevator to the tenth floor. The hallway smelled of copy toner and stale coffee. A clerk at the front desk barely looked up as they passed.

Room 1014 was already crowded: two health inspectors, a notary, and Cecilia Rau standing with her arms crossed, her white coat stark against the institutional beige.

“Dr. Rau,” Javier said. “You’re early.”

“As were you.” She didn’t smile. “The team’s ready.”

The lead inspector, a gaunt man named Flores, opened his briefcase. “We’re here to review Valente & Asociados for compliance with biosafety protocols and record-keeping standards.”

Javier nodded. “Proceed.”

The inspection took two hours. They walked through the empty clinic—ceramic tiles, stainless steel counters, the faint scent of disinfectant. Rivo’s team opened cabinets, pulled patient files, tested autoclave temperatures.

Cecilia followed, arms still crossed, watching.

At the end of the hallway, Flores paused. “Where are the narcotics logs?”

Dr. Rau stepped forward. “There are no narcotics on site. Dr. Valente’s practice is cardiac consulting only. EKGs, stress tests, echocardiograms.”

Flores didn’t look convinced. He turned to Javier. “That’s unusual for a clinic of this size.”

Javier’s jaw tightened. “It’s a boutique practice. Focused on preventative care.”

Flores wasn’t appeased. “Preventative care still requires controlled substances for emergencies.”

Rivas cleared his throat. “Perhaps Dr. Rau can clarify the formulary.”

Cecilia didn’t flinch. “We don’t stock controlled substances. Every referral requiring medication goes to Hospital Italiano. You can verify that with their pharmacy.”

Flores scribbled something in his notebook.

Javier watched the exchange. Something was off. Not wrong, but rehearsed.

They moved to the records room. Flores pulled random files, flipped through lab results, patient consent forms. Everything was immaculate.

“Admirable compliance,” he muttered.

Judge Marini, who had been quietly observing from a corner, finally spoke. “You expected to find something?”

Flores didn’t answer. He closed the last file. “This clinic is cleaner than a Swiss bank vault.”

Javier’s phone buzzed. A text from Laura: *Dinner is late. Drinks at Tortoni. Ten o’clock.*

He silenced it.

Cecilia stepped closer to Javier, her voice low. “You’re wasting your time. And mine.”

He turned, meeting her gaze. “Busy evening, Doctor. Settling into a new position?”

“More like covering old mistakes.” She glanced at Flores, then back. “Rigo’s not the man you think he is.”

Javier smiled. “And who do I think he is?”

“The ghost of the dictatorship.” She kept her voice steady, her eyes locked on his. “You’re trying to unearth a corpse that’s already buried.”

Javier leaned in slightly. “Corpses have a way of rising when you least expect them.”

Cecilia didn’t back down. “Not this one.”

Flores snapped his briefcase shut. “We’re done here.”

Javier exhaled through his nose. “Already?”

Flores handed him a slip of paper. “Regulatory violations: none found. Recommendation: routine follow-up in six months. Next inspection scheduled for March.”

Javier took the paper, crumpled it in his fist.

Marini chuckled. “You’re chasing ghosts, Javier.”

Rivas shifted uncomfortably. “Sir—”

“Don’t.” Javier’s voice cut through. “It’s not over.”

Cecilia’s eyes flickered—something like pity, or maybe satisfaction. She turned and walked away, her white coat disappearing into the hallway.

Javier waited until she was gone. “Flores, hand me that file on *Operation Clean Hands*.”

The inspector blinked. “Sir?”

“You heard me. The black ops ledger. It was in the stack.”

Flores hesitated. Then he reached into his briefcase and pulled out a manila folder. Inside was a single sheet of paper, stamped with official seals.

Javier took it. His breath hitched.

It wasn’t a list of names. It wasn’t even a report.

It was a single line:

**Case #742: Subject M.I. – Status: Terminated.**

His pulse roared in his ears.

That wasn’t possible. M.I. had been a juvenile asset—handled, debried, terminated. Officially.

But the paper was real. The stamp was real.

And the date—June 12, 1984—was real.

Javier’s hands shook.

Rivas leaned in. “Sir, what is it?”

Javier didn’t answer. He flipped the sheet over.

Nothing.

He reached for the folder again, rifling through the rest.

Empty.

Flores frowned. “That was the only document. The rest were clinic records.”

Javier’s mind raced. Who would plant this? Why?

Then it clicked.

Valente.

Of course.

The surgeon who’d once cut out hearts now dug up the dead.

Javier’s phone buzzed again. A message from an unknown number:

*Justice Albarracín. Your ledger is safe. But Mateo Ibarra’s article runs tomorrow. Tick tock.*

He deleted it.

Rivas was still watching. “Sir, are you alright?”

Javier crushed the paper in his fist. “I’m going to Tortoni.”

Rivas nodded. “Shall I drive?”

“No.” Javier walked toward the elevator, each step deliberate. “I need to walk.”

The doors dinged open. He stepped inside.

As the elevator descended, he unclenched his fist.

The paper fluttered to the floor, landing at Flores’ feet.

Javier didn’t look back.

But in the mirrored wall, he saw Inspector Rivas pick it up, unfold it, and read the single line.

His face went pale.

Chapter 6

The lease office on Calle Posadas smelled of old paper and lemon polish. Rigo adjusted his tie in the reflection of the mahogany counter. The clerk, a man in his fifties with a permanent squint, tapped his pen against a stack of folders.

"You sure about this?" the clerk asked.

"Just do it."

The clerk exhaled through his nose. "Offshore shell company. That’s a three-thousand-peso fee. Plus notary."

Rigo slid an envelope across the counter. The clerk weighed it in his hand, then tucked it into his breast pocket without counting.

"Owner name?"

"Nicolás Prado."

The clerk raised an eyebrow but didn’t question it. He typed at the keyboard, the screen flickering in the dim light. "Current tenant is Mateo Ibarra. Lease expires in six months."

"Transfer it."

The clerk paused. "He’ll need to sign off."

Rigo pulled a folded document from his inside pocket. A signature, dark and looping, matched Mateo’s name on the lease. "Already taken care of."

The clerk studied the signature. Not questioned—no one questioned these things in Buenos Aires, not when the envelope was the size of a deck of cards. He stamped the transfer form, slapped a new sticker on the file, and handed it back.

"Done."

Rigo took the file, tapped it against his palm. "One more thing."

The clerk sighed. "What now?"

"Evict the tenant."

The clerk blinked. "That’s not how this works. The new owner can terminate with thirty days’ notice, but—"

"Today."

The clerk rubbed his temples. "Señor Prado wants the apartment vacant by sundown?"

"Not Prado." Rigo slid a second envelope across the counter. Thicker. The clerk’s eyes flicked to it, then away.

"Terminate immediately," Rigo said. "Locks changed. Utilities cut after midnight."

The clerk licked his lips. "And the tenant?"

"Doesn’t matter."

The clerk swallowed, then folded the second envelope into his pocket. "You’re a son of a bitch, Valente."

Rigo didn’t react. He turned toward the door.

"Wait." The clerk called after him. "The tenant might have—"

Rigo kept walking.

Outside, the afternoon sun glared off the pavement. Rigo lit a cigarette, cupped the flame in his hands. The phone in his pocket buzzed. He ignored it, exhaled smoke through his nose.

The phone buzzed again.

He pulled it out, thumb hovering over the screen. The display read: *Unknown. 1 new message.*

He opened it.

*"This is Inés. We need to talk."*

Rigo’s thumb hovered. He didn’t save the number. Didn’t reply.

He took a long drag, flicked ash onto the sidewalk. The phone buzzed a third time.

He powered it off, slipped it back into his pocket.

A block away, a payphone rang. Rigo didn’t look back.

Chapter 7

The Senate hearing room smelled of polished wood and stale coffee. Javier Albarracín sat ramrod straight in his tailored suit, fingers laced over the mahogany table, eyes fixed on the witness—some mid-level prosecutor from the ’78 archives. The man cleared his throat.

"A classified operation under Military Justice Decree 1087 was initially attributed to... certain elements within the Buenos Aires medical corps. Later, those same elements were quietly absorbed into private clinics under new names. Valente & Asociados, for example."

A ripple passed through the room. Javier’s jaw tightened.

Senator Albarracín—his father, a gaunt man with a senator’s perpetual squint—shifted in his seat. "Are you suggesting a conspiracy?"

"Not suggesting, Senator. Documenting." The witness tapped a file. "And the clinic in question? It’s a front."

Javier’s left hand twitched. He folded it under the table.

Cecilia Rau sat two rows back, elbows on the railing, chin in her palm. Her pager vibrated against her hip. She glanced down, then paled.

The address scrolled across the screen: *Quinta Las Azoteas – Midnight.*

Javier’s aide leaned in. "Justice, we should recess."

"Not now." Javier’s voice carried across the room.

"Sir—"

"Later."

The aide hesitated, then nodded and scribbled a note.

Cecilia’s fingers drummed the railing. The pager buzzed again. She slipped it into her bag, stood, and moved toward the aisle.

A journalist in the back row caught her eye and gave a slight nod.

She walked out without looking back.

---

Outside, the evening air carried the scent of jasmine and diesel fumes. Cecilia hailed a taxi, rattled off the address as she fastened her seatbelt.

The driver glanced in the rearview mirror. "You meeting someone at the estancia?"

"Who else would send me there?"

The driver smirked. "Business or pleasure?"

"Neither."

The taxi accelerated into traffic.

---

The gates of Quinta Las Azoteas stood open, lanterns bleeding gold across the gravel drive. Cecilia’s heels crunched on crushed stone as she approached the front door. The house loomed—whitewashed walls, terracotta tiles, ivy crawling up the pillars.

She knocked once.

The door opened before the echo faded. A man in a butler’s coat stood there, silhouetted against the warm glow of the foyer.

"Dr. Rau?"

"Yes."

"Please, come in."

She stepped inside. The scent of aged leather and aged wine wrapped around her. The butler closed the door behind her.

"Justice Albarracín isn’t available tonight," the man said. "But he left instructions for your arrival."

Cecilia’s pulse thrummed in her throat. "Where is he?"

"He’s unavoidably detained in the city. He asked that you wait in the library."

She followed the butler down a corridor lined with antique maps. The library door stood ajar. Floor-to-ceiling shelves groaned under the weight of leather-bound books.

"Wine?" The butler gestured to a silver tray.

"No, thank you."

He poured anyway. "Justice Albarracín’s preference. A ’82 Malbec."

Cecilia didn’t touch it. She walked to the far shelf, running her fingers along the spines.

The butler placed the glass on a side table. "I’ll be in the kitchen if you need anything."

She nodded, waited until his footsteps faded, then turned back to the shelves.

Between Borges and Bioy Casares, a narrow gap yawned. She knelt, brushed her fingers over the wood.

A floorboard shifted.

She froze.

The wine glass shattered against the hardwood.

Rigo Valente stepped from the shadows, a pistol in his right hand, his left pressing a finger to his lips.

Cecilia’s breath hitched.

He didn’t smile. Didn’t apologize.

She stared at the gun, then at his face.

"You," she said.

Chapter 8

Mateo’s apartment smelled like burnt coffee and old takeout. The laptop sat open on the kitchenette table, screen glowing. He hadn’t even noticed it was gone.

Rigo stepped inside without knocking. The door swung shut behind him.

Mateo spun around, a half-eaten sandwich in hand. "What the hell—"

"Your laptop. Where is it?"

"Relax. Nicolás borrowed it. Said he needed to—"

"Nicolás Prado?" Rigo’s voice dropped. "When?"

"Yesterday. He said it was urgent."

Rigo exhaled through his nose. "And you let him walk out with it."

Mateo tossed the sandwich onto the counter. "What’s the big deal? It’s just a fucking laptop."

Rigo moved to the window. The street below was a slow river of headlights and honking taxis. "You signed a nondisclosure agreement. Nicolás Prado is connected to the Albarracíns. That machine holds everything you’ve dug up on my operations. Everything *we* dug up."

Mateo’s smirk faded. "You don’t get to call it *we*."

Rigo didn’t turn. "Where does Prado live?"

"Like I’d tell you."

Rigo picked up a pen from the table. A receipt from Café Tortoni fluttered underneath. He held it up. "You were there this morning. Talking to an old man at the bar. What did you ask him?"

Mateo crossed his arms. "So?"

"So the old man was dead by noon."

Silence.

Then Mateo laughed. "You’re paranoid. The dude was ninety if he was a day."

Rigo pocketed the receipt. "Where. Does. Prado. Live."

Mateo stared at the dark screen of his laptop. "Puerto Madero. High-rise on the river. Unit 2401." He said it like a surrender.

Rigo was already at the door. "Lock this behind me."

---

The El Tobogán Club’s marble floors were slick with spilled champagne and cigar ash. Inés stood at the bar, fingers wrapped around a glass of torrontés she hadn’t touched. The hum of conversation wrapped around her like a shroud.

Javier Albarracín appeared at her side without a sound. He was in a charcoal suit, his silver tie perfectly knotted, his expression unreadable.

"Inés," he said. "You look like you’ve seen a ghost."

She didn’t look at him. "I just got a call from the Ministry. My security clearance’s been revoked."

Javier sighed, almost amused. "Cultural attaché isn’t a national security position. That’s all this is."

"It’s not just that." She set the glass down. "They mentioned ‘sensitive files.’ Said I’d breached protocol."

Javier’s fingers brushed her wrist. She pulled away.

"Then we’ll get you a new post," he said. "Ambassador to Uruguay. Quiet. Dignified."

"Or I could just resign."

His smile didn’t reach his eyes. "After the confirmation vote? It would look like panic."

A waiter approached, bowed slightly. "Justice Albarracín, your aide has submitted his resignation."

Javier’s jaw tightened. "What?"

"He left a letter on your desk. Says ‘moral compromise.’"

Inés watched Javier’s face go still. Then, slowly, he laughed. A low, dangerous sound.

"Diego’s a fool," he said. "A sentimental fool."

The waiter hesitated. "Shall I send for him?"

Javier waved a hand. "No. Let him stew."

Inés finally turned to him. "You knew, didn’t you? About the files?"

Javier’s gaze flicked to her. "About what?"

"About whatever Mateo’s digging up. About Rigo Valente."

Javier’s expression darkened. "Inés, you have to—"

Her pager buzzed. She flinched. The screen read: *Mateo’s apartment. Now.*

She didn’t wait for an explanation. She left the glass on the bar and walked out.

Javier didn’t try to stop her.

---

The doorman at Mateo’s building barely glanced up as Inés stormed past. The elevator smelled like disinfectant and old sweat. She took the stairs instead, three at a time.

Mateo’s door was unlocked.

Inside, the apartment was a mess—clothes on the floor, dishes in the sink, the faint scent of burnt wiring lingering in the air. Mateo wasn’t there.

But his desk was.

The draft of his next article sat open on the screen:

**"The Ghost of '78: How a Surgeon Became the Nightmare of the Disappeared"**

Inés’s breath caught. She reached for the mouse, scrolled through the lines. Names. Dates. Photos of surgical instruments labeled in faded handwriting.

Then she saw it.

In the margin, in Mateo’s hurried scrawl:

*"Ask about the Borges book."*

A floorboard creaked behind her.

She turned.

Rigo Valente stood in the doorway, his hands in his pockets, his face unreadable.

Inés didn’t move. "You."

Rigo stepped inside. Closed the door behind him.

"What are you doing here?" she asked.

He didn’t answer. His eyes flicked to the screen.

She slammed the laptop shut.

"Don’t touch my brother’s work," she said.

Rigo’s gaze locked onto hers. "He’s digging up graves, Inés."

She stepped closer. "And you’re the ghost in them."

A long silence stretched between them.

Then Rigo’s phone buzzed. He pulled it out, glanced at the screen, and pocketed it again without answering.

Inés’s own phone rang. Unknown number.

She ignored it.

Rigo took a step toward her. "You need to leave. Now."

She didn’t move. "Or what?"

He didn’t answer that either.

The phone buzzed again. Persistent. Demanding.

Inés finally fished it out. Swiped to answer.

A voice, distorted, mechanical: "Tell Valente the ledger isn’t enough. Tell him the boy knows where he hides." Click.

The line went dead.

Inés stared at the screen. Then up at Rigo.

His face had gone pale.

"Who was that?" she asked.

Rigo’s jaw worked. "No one."

But his hands were shaking.

Chapter 9

The clinic was dark when Cecilia Rau stepped through the door.

Rigo sat behind his desk, the only light coming from the emergency exit sign above the door. He didn’t look up as she walked in. The scent of her perfume—something citrus and sharp—cut through the sterile air.

"You’re still here," she said.

"Always am."

She placed a file on his desk. The leather-bound cover gleamed under the dim light. "I know about the ledger."

Rigo exhaled through his nose. His fingers tapped once against the desk, a slow, steady rhythm. "Do you?"

"Yes." Cecilia opened the file. Inside were photocopies of pages—names, dates, transactions. All in Rigo’s handwriting. "You kept it all these years. Why?"

"Because some debts aren’t meant to be forgotten."

She flicked a page. "And some debts are meant to bury you."

Rigo leaned back in his chair. The springs groaned. "Is that an offer?"

Cecilia closed the file. "Come back to the hospital. Help me bury this. I’ll handle the psychiatric evaluation. No questions. No records." She slid the Borges first edition across the desk. "Take this. It’s yours now."

Rigo didn’t touch it. "Why?"

"Because I trust you. More than I trust the board. More than I trust myself right now." Her voice cracked on the last word.

He picked up the book. The leather was cracked at the spine, the gold lettering faded. He flipped through the pages, slow and deliberate. "You think this changes anything?"

"It changes everything." She stepped closer. "You’re not the ghost of ’78, Rigo. You’re not El Fantasma to me."

He set the book down. "And what am I to you now?"

"Someone worth saving."

Rigo stood. His chair rolled back with a sharp squeak. He walked to the window. The street below was empty, the buildings silent. "Saving implies I need it."

"You do."

He turned. "You don’t know what you’re asking."

"I know exactly what I’m asking." Cecilia’s hands were steady. "I know about the ledger. I know about Mateo. I know you’ve been running for fifteen years because you think you failed him."

Rigo’s jaw tightened. "You don’t know a goddamn thing."

"I know you’re not the monster Javier thinks you are." She took a step forward. "But you’re close. And if you don’t let me help you, you’ll cross that line. You already have."

Rigo picked up the Borges book again. Held it to his chest. "And what’s your price, Cecilia?"

"One thing." She met his gaze. "The truth. Just once. Before it’s all over."

He stared at her for a long moment. Then he set the book down. Walked past her. Stopped at the door.

She turned as he spoke.

"Take the book," Rigo said. "It’s yours now."

Cecilia reached for it. "Rigo—"

He was already gone. The door clicked shut behind him.

She stood there, the book in her hands, the silence pressing in around her. Then she turned to the window.

Rigo’s figure moved through the street below, a shadow swallowed by the night.

Cecilia held the book tighter.

Then she paused.

She looked back at the desk. The ledger was still there. Open. A single page highlighted.

She stepped forward, flipped it over.

The name at the top made her breath catch.

*Javier Albarracín.*

Not his father’s. His.

Cecilia’s fingers trembled as she closed the ledger. She tucked it under her arm, the Borges book tucked into her bag.

Then she walked to the door.

Paused.

And said, "Mateo isn’t who you think he is."

Chapter 10

Rigo woke to the sound of rain on glass.

He lay on his office couch, fully clothed, the Borges book pressed against his chest like a shield. The pages smelled of leather and old paper and something else—damp earth, the river after a storm. He hadn’t slept in the apartment above the clinic in weeks. Tonight, he hadn’t slept at all.

The rain blurred the neon sign outside his window—*Valente & Asociados* flickered like a dying pulse. He sat up, joints protesting. The ledger lay open on the coffee table, its pages fanned like wings. He hadn’t meant to leave it out. He never did.

A knock at the door.

Rigo didn’t move. The knock came again, sharper this time. He exhaled, smoothed the pages shut, and slipped the book into his briefcase. He’d leave it somewhere else, somewhere safer than a clinic that hummed with hidden eyes.

The knock turned into a fist.

“It’s open,” he said.

The door swung wide. Cecilia stood there, soaked from the rain, her dark hair plastered to her neck. She held a manila envelope in one hand, her bag in the other. Her coat dripped onto the linoleum.

“You look like hell,” she said.

“Feeling worse.”

She stepped inside, shut the door with her hip. “You didn’t answer your phone.”

“I turn it off when I sleep.”

“You don’t sleep.”

Rigo stood, brushed past her to the window. The rain sheeted down, turning the street into a shimmering blur. “What do you want, Cecilia?”

“To understand.” She dropped the envelope onto his desk. Water beaded on the leather. “I spent twenty minutes in the archives today. Cross-referencing names. Dates. Everything lines up.”

He didn’t touch the envelope. “What lines up?”

“You.” She tapped the envelope. “El Fantasma wasn’t just a ghost. You were everywhere. Operations in Córdoba. Safe houses in Mendoza. A black site in Mar del Plata where they waterboarded priests.” Her voice didn’t waver. “And then you disappeared. Just like that.”

Rigo walked to the coffee maker, poured himself a cup of cold dregs. “Disappearing’s an art. You should try it.”

“Not yet.” She pulled a file from her bag, flipped it open. Photos spilled out—heads bent, voices low, hands clasped across tables in El Tobogán’s private rooms. “These were taken last month. Justice Albarracín’s inner circle. Every single one of them was on your ledger.”

Rigo didn’t look. “You followed them?”

“I followed you.” She set the file down. “You were there too. In the background. Watching. Waiting.”

He turned, back against the counter. “People watch. It’s what they do.”

“Not like this.” Cecilia closed the file with a snap. “You were cataloging them. For blackmail. For leverage. For revenge.”

Rigo set the cup down. The dregs sloshed over his fingers. “Revenge is a luxury, Cecilia. I can’t afford it.”

She stepped closer. “Then what was this? Fifteen years of silence. Now suddenly you’re back. Now suddenly you’re keeping names.” Her eyes locked onto the briefcase. “What’s in the book, Rigo?”

He zipped the briefcase shut. “Nothing you need to know.”

“It’s the ledger.” She said it like a verdict. “You copied it. You hid it. You’re using it as bait.”

Rigo moved to the door, briefcase in hand. “You’re overexcited. It’s burnout. You said that yourself.”

Cecilia blocked the door. “I read the names, Rigo. I read the transactions. I read the single page you left open on your desk like an invitation.” She was close enough to smell the rain on her coat, the sharp citrus of her perfume beneath. “Javier Albarracín’s father. A dictator’s cousin. A military judge who signed death warrants. All of them paid. All of them protected. And now Javier is asking for the same confirmation that put his father’s generation in power.”

Rigo didn’t flinch. “He’s asking for justice.”

“He’s asking for control.” She lowered her voice. “And you’re giving him a knife to cut his own throat.”

Rigo looked past her, through the rain-streaked window. The city pulsed with neon and headlights, a living circuit board of deals and denials. “I gave him a ledger. He can read it. He can ignore it. He can burn it. It doesn’t matter.”

“It does.” Cecilia’s voice cracked. “Because you’re not the architect of silence anymore. You’re the seamstress.” She touched the briefcase. “You’re stitching the past back together so someone else can wear it like a uniform.”

Rigo caught her wrist, gentle but firm. “Don’t.”

She didn’t pull away. “Don’t what? Tell the truth? Or tell you that you’re still playing God?”

His grip tightened just enough to feel her pulse. “I’m not playing.”

“Then what are you doing?” She leaned in. “You think Mateo Ibarra is the boy you failed fifteen years ago? You think Inés still loves the ghost you pretended to be? You think—”

Rigo let go. Took a step back. “Don’t.”

Cecilia’s breath hitched. She pressed her fingertips to her mouth, as if she could push the words back inside.

Silence wedged between them, thick as the rain outside.

Then Rigo spoke, low and measured. “Mateo is not my son.”

Cecilia exhaled. “Then who is he?”

Rigo hesitated. Just for a second. Just long enough.

Cecilia’s eyes narrowed. She reached into her bag, pulled out a folded piece of paper. A newspaper clipping. Mateo’s byline. A photo of a boy, fifteen years younger, standing in front of a burned-out building.

“Read it,” she said.

Rigo didn’t take it. “I don’t need to.”

“You do.” She unfolded the page. A paragraph was circled in red. “Listen: *‘The child survived. But the man who saved him vanished before dawn.’*”

Rigo’s fingers twitched. His briefcase slipped from his grip, landing with a thud on the linoleum. The sound echoed.

Cecilia kept reading. “*‘His name was El Fantasma. He left no trace. Not even a ghost.’*”

Rigo’s jaw clenched. “Where did you get this?”

“From Mateo’s draft.” She held the paper closer. “It’s going to print tomorrow. He’s naming you, Rigo. Not as a monster. As a savior.”

Rigo looked at the briefcase. At the floor. At the rain streaking the window.

Then he looked at Cecilia. Really looked.

She was trembling.

“You knew,” he said quietly.

“I suspected.” Her voice broke. “But I needed to hear you say it.”

Rigo picked up the briefcase. The ledger was inside. The names. The dates. The money. The deals. And one name, highlighted in red:

*Mateo Ibarra.*

Not his son.

But his failure.

He zipped it shut.

Cecilia took a shaky breath. “What are you going to do?”

Rigo walked to the door. Put his hand on the knob.

“Whatever it takes,” he said.

And he walked out into the rain.

Chapter 11

Mateo pressed his palm against the café’s glass door, the wet heat of his breath fogging the pane. Inside, Tortoni’s hummed with the late-night din of lawyers swapping gossip and journalists typing up their last pieces before deadline. He adjusted the strap of his satchel, the weight of the draft heavy against his hip. The article was ready. Tomorrow it would run. Tonight, he just wanted coffee.

The door swung open before he could pull it.

A man in a raincoat stood there, face shadowed under a crumpled fedora. He grabbed Mateo’s wrist, yanked him into the alley beside the café. The door banged shut behind them.

“You think this is smart?” The man’s voice was rough, familiar. Nicolás Prado.

Mateo twisted free. “Get your hands off me.”

Nicolás stepped closer, the alley’s single bulb casting long shadows. “You’ve been following me. Following *her*. You don’t know what you’re playing with.”

“I know exactly what I’m playing with.” Mateo’s fingers tightened around his satchel. “Rigo’s past. Your lies. The truth.”

Nicolás laughed, low and wet. “Truth? You don’t even know what that looks like.” He reached into his coat.

Mateo didn’t wait. He drove his knee up hard. Nicolás grunted, doubled over. Mateo bolted for the alley’s mouth.

A car engine growled. Tires screeched. A black sedan skidded to a stop across the alley’s exit. Two men in dark suits spilled out, automatic pistols glinting in the dim light.

Mateo’s breath caught.

Nicolás staggered upright, wiping blood from his lip. “Told you,” he said. “You don’t get to walk away.”

One of the men grabbed Mateo’s arm, twisted it behind his back. The other pressed the pistol to his temple.

Mateo’s pulse roared in his ears.

A gunshot cracked.

Not at him.

The man holding the pistol jerked, blood blooming across his shoulder. He staggered back, shock twisting his face. The second man spun, pistol up.

Mateo dropped to the pavement.

A third figure stepped into the alley’s light. Tall. Unhurried. Rigo Valente, rain slick on his shoulders, a smoking pistol in his hand.

Nicolás cursed, backing into the shadows.

Rigo’s gaze locked onto Mateo. “You okay?”

Mateo’s chest heaved. “What the hell are you doing?”

Rigo holstered the gun. “Saving your life.” He reached for Mateo’s arm, hauled him up. “They’re not here for you. They’re here for me.”

Mateo wrenched free. “Bullshit. You set me up last week. You had Nicolás steal my laptop. You’re the reason I’ve got jack shit on Inés right now.”

Rigo’s expression didn’t change. “You want proof? Look.” He nodded toward the sedan.

The passenger door opened. A woman stepped out, her blonde hair damp, her coat clinging to her frame. Laura Albarracín. Justice Javier Albarracín’s wife.

She held a cigarette, unlit. Her fingers trembled.

Rigo’s voice dropped. “She was giving orders. Not Nicolás. Not these men.” He nodded at the two bleeding gangsters, now cuffed and kneeling in the alley muck. “Justice Albarracín’s private security detail. All of them reporting to his wife.”

Mateo’s stomach twisted. “Why?”

Rigo’s eyes flicked to Laura. She lifted the cigarette to her lips, bit down. No smoke. Just the habit.

“Because,” Rigo said, “she thinks you know too much.”

Laura raised her head. Her gaze locked onto Mateo. She didn’t speak. Didn’t need to. The threat hung in the air, sharp as the rain.

Mateo’s pulse hammered.

Rigo’s hand settled on his shoulder. “We need to move. Now.”

Mateo shrugged him off. The draft in his satchel felt heavier than ever. “I’m not running.”

Rigo’s jaw tightened. “Then you’re dead.”

A voice cut through the tension.

“Drop the gun, Doctor. Hands where I can see them.”

Mateo turned.

A police captain stood at the alley’s mouth, pistol drawn, flanked by four uniformed officers. Their radios crackled with static.

Rigo didn’t move. “Captain Mendez. Your timing’s impeccable.”

Mendez’s eyes flicked to Mateo, then to Laura, then back to Rigo. His jaw worked. “Justice Albarracín called this in himself. Attempted murder, obstruction, conspiracy.” His pistol didn’t waver. “Step away from the civilian.”

Mateo’s breath came fast. “Rigo—”

Rigo’s hands rose, slow. “Mateo, get out of here. Go to Inés. Tell her—”

“Shut up,” Mendez snapped. “Both of you.”

Laura exhaled, finally lighting her cigarette. The flare of the lighter was the only warmth in the cold alley.

Rigo’s gaze never left Mendez’s. “You really want to do this here? Now?”

Mendez’s finger tightened on the trigger. “Stand down, Doctor. Or we’ll do it for you.”

Rigo’s lips parted. Almost a smile. Almost resignation.

Then he moved.

Not toward the police. Not toward Laura.

He grabbed Mateo’s arm, yanked him close. “Run.”

Mateo stumbled, caught off balance. The alley’s mouth was twenty feet away, the gap between cops and safety narrowing with every second.

Rigo didn’t follow.

Mateo turned.

Rigo stood between him and the cops, hands still raised, but his stance shifting—feet planted, shoulders squared. A human shield.

Mendez’s voice boomed. “Last warning.”

Rigo didn’t flinch. “You want me, Captain? Come take me.”

Mendez’s jaw clenched. He raised his pistol higher.

Then the radio at his belt squawked. A voice, urgent. “Captain, we’ve got a situation at Justice Albarracín’s residence. Urgent. Override code black.”

Mendez’s eyes flicked to Laura. She flicked her cigarette ash onto the wet pavement, her expression unreadable.

The moment’s hesitation was enough.

Rigo lunged toward Mateo, shoving him backward. “Go!”

Mateo bolted.

The alley’s mouth loomed. The rain sheeted down, turning the street into a shimmering blur of neon and headlights. He didn’t look back.

Behind him, Mendez barked orders. Pistol shots cracked. Not at Mateo. Not at him.

Rigo’s voice cut through the chaos, sharp and clear. “Get him out of here!”

Mateo didn’t stop running until he hit Avenida Corrientes, his lungs burning, his shoes slapping wet pavement. He ducked into a recessed doorway, pressed his back against the wall. His hands shook.

He pulled his phone, dialed Inés.

No answer.

He tried again. Same result.

A car’s headlights swept past. Mateo’s breath hitched. He ducked lower, heart hammering.

In the distance, sirens wailed.

Mateo’s mind raced. Rigo. Laura. The cops. The gunshots. The draft burning in his satchel.

He needed to get out of sight. Needed to find Inés.

Needed to understand what the hell was happening.

He looked up.

Across the street, a café’s windows glowed warm. Inside, figures hunched over laptops, sipping coffee, oblivious.

Mateo stepped into the rain, moving fast.

Behind him, another set of headlights turned the corner.

He didn’t look back.

He couldn’t.

Not yet.

Chapter 12

The Judicial Archives of Buenos Aires smelled of damp paper and lemon oil. Rigo adjusted his cufflinks as he walked down the dim corridor, his dress shoes clicking against the polished tile. The motion-activated lights flickered to life one by one, casting long shadows ahead of him. He carried nothing but a worn leather folio tucked under his arm.

Javier Albarracín stood by the reading table, his back to the door. He was arranging a stack of leather-bound case files, his movements precise, measured. The Supreme Court justice’s suit was immaculate, the jacket draped over the back of a chair like he’d been there for hours.

“You’re late,” Javier said without turning.

Rigo stopped a dozen feet away. “You’re predictable.”

Javier exhaled through his nose, finally pivoting. His eyes were cold, calculating. “I prefer *methodical*.” His gaze flicked to the folio. “You brought it?”

“You sent your wife to kill a man in broad daylight.” Rigo set the folio on the table. The impact was soft, almost delicate. “That’s not methodical. That’s desperate.”

Javier’s fingers twitched. “Mateo Ibarra pushed too far. He knew things he shouldn’t.”

“He knew *you* shouldn’t have. Or did you forget the arms deal?” Rigo unlatched the folio. Inside, the microfilm reel glinted in the overhead light. “Your father’s signature on the FMS contract. Paid for by dirty money. Funds that built this city’s ‘reforms’.” He tapped the reel. “I have the original. The only copy.”

Javier’s face didn’t change. “You’re bluffing.”

Rigo reached into his breast pocket. Pulled out a small recorder. “I didn’t come here to bluff.”

Javier’s jaw clenched. “What is that?”

“Something Mateo said.” Rigo pressed play.

A voice crackled from the tiny speaker, rough with static and distance.

*I just wanted to know who my father was.*

Silence followed. Javier’s complexion paled. His fingers curled into fists.

Rigo killed the recording. “Recognize the voice?”

Javier didn’t answer. He turned, walked to the window. The stained glass cast a kaleidoscope of colors across the marble floor. “Mateo Ibarra is nothing to me.”

“He’s your son.”

The words landed like a thrown stone. Javier’s shoulders locked. “You’re lying.”

“Am I?” Rigo stepped forward. “I knew your wife in ’84. Laura was pregnant. Rounding errors in hospital records. A stillborn in Córdoba. Convenient timing.” He opened the folio wider. A photograph slid out—blurred, grainy. A young woman in a hospital gown, holding a newborn. “This was taken the day Mateo was born. Inés’ birthday is six months later. You really think she’s yours?”

Javier’s hand shot out, snatched the photo. His breath hitched. “This is—”

“Proof. Or would be, if you cared to look.” Rigo closed the folio. “Mateo doesn’t know. Neither does Inés. But you did. Or at least, Laura did.” He watched Javier carefully. “She orchestrated the hit tonight because Mateo found the adoption papers. Because he was getting too close to the truth. Because he’s your son, Javier. And you let her try to kill him.”

Javier’s face was a mask of controlled fury. “You expect me to believe any of this?”

“Believe whatever keeps you sleeping at night.” Rigo’s voice dropped. “But remember this—when the Senate votes on your confirmation, when the press digs into your father’s legacy, when Mateo’s article drops tomorrow, *this* will be the thing that undoes you.” He tapped the folio again. “Your father’s blood money. Your wife’s crimes. Your son’s survival. All of it in one neat package.”

Javier’s hands trembled. For the first time, Rigo saw the man beneath the robes—the one who’d spent his career polishing a reputation he never earned. “You want money? A deal?”

“No.” Rigo picked up the folio. “I want you to resign. Publicly. Before the vote. Before Mateo publishes. Before the world sees what you really are.”

Javier’s laugh was sharp, bitter. “And if I don’t?”

Rigo met his gaze. “Then I walk into *El Tobogán* tomorrow night. Hand this reel to every editor and judge in attendance. Let them decide if a Supreme Court justice should be presiding over cases when his father sold weapons to the dictatorship.” He tucked the folio under his arm. “Your call, Javier. But make it fast.”

Javier’s chest rose and fell. “You think this is over?”

“It ends tonight.” Rigo turned toward the door. “Or it ends with you in prison.”

“Rigo.”

He paused.

“Why?” Javier’s voice was quieter now, stripped of its usual authority. “After all these years. Why now?”

Rigo looked back. “Because Mateo deserves to know who his father is.” He didn’t wait for a response. He walked out, the echoes of his footsteps swallowed by the cavernous archives.

The door clicked shut behind him.

In the hallway, he leaned against the wall for a moment, exhaling. The weight in his chest wasn’t relief. It was something heavier. Something like dread.

He needed to get to Inés. Needed to tell her the truth—before Mateo did.

Before Javier did.

He pushed off the wall and reached for his phone.

The screen lit up. A missed call. Inés.

He dialed back, pressed the phone to his ear.

No answer.

He tried again.

Same result.

Rigo cursed under his breath, quickened his pace. He needed to find her. Now.

The elevator dinged at the end of the hall.

Rigo rounded the corner—

—and froze.

Inés stood there, her coat damp from the rain, her eyes wide. Behind her, two men in surgical greens stood shoulder to shoulder, blocking the exit. One held a syringe.

Rigo’s blood turned to ice.

Inés’s lips parted. “I was hoping I’d catch you here.”

Chapter 13

The Borges bookstore on Corrientes smelled of old paper and lemon polish. Rigo paused inside the doorway, letting his eyes adjust. The aisles were narrow, lined with shelves that reached to the ceiling. In the back, by the fireplace, a single reading chair faced the flames.

Inés stood there.

She wore a dark wool coat, damp at the shoulders from the sudden downpour outside. Her gloved hands clutched a small envelope. When she saw him, she didn’t smile.

Rigo stepped forward. “Inés.”

“You knew.” Her voice was low, controlled. “You knew all along.”

The fire crackled. A customer browsing the poetry section glanced over, then quickly away.

Rigo didn’t answer right away. He moved closer, close enough to see the silver locket at her throat—something she’d worn since she was a girl. “Knew what?”

“Don’t.” She held up the envelope. “I found this in Mateo’s apartment. The microfilm reel. The one from the Borges edition you gave him.” She shook her head. “It’s Javier’s father. On the arms deal. The same deal you used to blackmail him tonight.”

Rigo exhaled through his nose. “You read it?”

“I didn’t have to.” She stepped closer, close enough that he could see the dark circles under her eyes. “The metadata. The date. The names. It’s all there.” She dropped the envelope into the fire.

The microfilm curled as it touched flame. Black edges curled inward, then darkened. Rigo watched it burn, the burn pattern spreading like spilled ink.

“Inés—”

“You lied to me.” Her voice cracked once, sharp as a snapped twig. “You lied to him. You let him think you abandoned him. You let him think he was nothing.”

The fire hissed.

Rigo looked at her. Really looked. The tilt of her chin, the way her fingers curled into fists inside her gloves. The locket swayed with her pulse.

“I tried to save him,” he said. “I failed.”

Inés’s eyes burned. “You *tried*? That’s not saving. That’s just another kind of abandonment.”

Rigo didn’t flinch. “I pulled him out of the safe house in ’84. Got him to a priest in Córdoba. Told him to stay hidden. But he didn’t stay. He came back. He wanted to know who he was. He wanted proof.” He met her gaze. “He still does.”

Inés’s breath hitched. “You could have told me.”

“And what would that have changed?” He stepped closer, close enough to catch the scent of rain and verbena. “You were fourteen. I was twenty-eight. I was El Fantasma. Your brother was just a kid who got caught in the middle.” He gestured at the burning film. “That’s not justice. That’s just another fire.”

Inés watched the microfilm dissolve into ash. “Javier’s going to resign, isn’t he?”

Rigo didn’t answer.

“He’ll resign. Publicly. Confess his father’s crimes. And you’ll keep the ledger hidden.” She laughed, a brittle sound. “So typical. You still control everything. Even the truth.”

Rigo reached into his coat pocket. Pulled out a small silver lighter. He flicked it open, watched the flame dance.

Inés’s eyes flicked to it. “What’s that?”

“Something else.” He snapped it shut. “Something I kept.”

She didn’t move. “You really think this ends now?”

Rigo didn’t answer.

Inés stared at the ashes. The firelight painted her face in flickering gold and shadow. “This changes nothing,” she said. She turned and walked toward the door, her coat whispering against the stacks.

Rigo didn’t follow.

The bookstore door chimed as she left.

He stood there, watching the fire eat the last of the microfilm. The envelope collapsed into gray flakes. The flames licked upward, hungry.

Rigo closed his eyes.

He should burn the ledger.

He should disappear.

But he didn’t move.

Somewhere beyond the bookstore, the city hummed. Somewhere, Inés was getting into a taxi. Somewhere, Javier was listening to the news, waiting for the resignation letter to drop.

Rigo opened his eyes.

The fire was almost out.

He reached into his coat again. Not for the lighter this time.

For the phone.

He dialed.

On the third ring, someone answered.

“Dr. Valente,” said a smooth, unfamiliar voice. “We’ve been expecting your call.”

Chapter 14

The Senate ethics hearing room smelled of polished mahogany and old pipe tobacco. Rigo sat at the long table, hands flat on the grain, fingers slightly spread as if anchoring himself to the wood. The committee members sat in a horseshoe, their faces a study in controlled neutrality. Javier was not in the room. He was watching through a closed-circuit feed in a private office down the hall, his lawyer hovering at his elbow.

The chairman, Senator Luzuriaga, cleared his throat. "Dr. Valente, you requested this meeting. State your terms."

Rigo didn’t look up. He folded his hands. "I want Javier Albarracín to resign. Publicly. Today. Before the confirmation vote."

A murmur rippled through the room. Luzuriaga’s jaw tightened. "That’s not within this committee’s power."

"Then I’ll make it within yours." Rigo slid a letter across the table. It was sealed with the Valente & Asociados letterhead. "This is my resignation from the medical board. Effective immediately."

Silence.

Luzuriaga picked up the letter, scanned it, then set it down with deliberate slowness. "You’re threatening to burn your own reputation? For what?"

"Not my reputation." Rigo met his eyes. "His."

The door to the side chamber opened. Javier’s lawyer stepped in, whispered something to Luzuriaga, then retreated. The chairman exhaled through his nose. "He’s listening."

"Good." Rigo didn’t smile. "Here’s the trade. Javier resigns. He confesses his father’s crimes—arms deals, disappearances, the whole rotten legacy. In exchange, I seal the ledger I’ve kept for thirty years. No one else ever sees the names inside. The blackmail stays buried."

Luzuriaga’s fingers drummed once on the table. "And what stops you from publishing it anyway?"

Rigo tapped the letter. "My word. As a surgeon."

The chairman leaned back. "That’s a thin guarantee."

Rigo stood. The chair legs scraped against the floor like a blade being drawn. "It’s the only one you’re going to get."

He turned toward the door.

"Wait."

Rigo paused.

Luzuriaga gestured to a side door. "Javier’s in the antechamber. He wants to speak to you directly."

Rigo didn’t move. "No."

"He asked for it."

Rigo turned slowly. "He asked. I didn’t."

The chairman’s phone buzzed. He glanced at it, then back at Rigo. "It’s him. He’s on the line now."

Rigo exhaled through his nose. He walked to the side door, pushed it open without waiting for an invitation.

Javier stood inside, phone in hand, his suit immaculate, his face unreadable. The room was small, carpeted in deep blue, lit by a single brass lamp. He didn’t hang up. He just waited.

Rigo stepped inside. The door clicked shut behind him.

Javier didn’t offer a hand. "You always were dramatic, El Fantasma."

Rigo didn’t answer.

Javier pocketed the phone. "You think this ends with me resigning?"

"Doesn’t end with anything." Rigo crossed his arms. "Just keeps the rot from spreading."

Javier laughed, a dry sound. "You don’t know what you’re protecting."

"I know what I’m not." Rigo’s voice was quiet. "I’m not protecting the past."

Silence stretched between them, taut as a wire.

Javier’s smile faded. "You think Mateo won’t publish without your ledger?"

Rigo didn’t react.

"He will. He’s not you. He doesn’t know when to quit. He’ll burn everything down just to feel the heat."

Rigo’s jaw tightened. "Then he’ll get what he wants."

Javier tilted his head. "You still think he wants justice?"

Rigo said nothing.

Javier stepped closer. "He wants blood. And you’re the one who gave it to him."

Rigo didn’t flinch.

Javier exhaled, slow. "Resign. Confess. I’ll do it. But you don’t get to walk away clean. Not this time."

Rigo turned toward the door. "Then we have an accord."

Javier’s voice dropped. "One more thing."

Rigo paused.

"You don’t know everything." Javier’s fingers flexed at his side. "You don’t know who Mateo really is."

Rigo looked at him. Really looked.

Javier held his gaze. Then he whispered something to his lawyer.

Rigo walked out.

Back in the hearing room, Luzuriaga stood, phone to ear. He hung up, met Rigo’s eyes. "He’s accepted your terms."

Rigo nodded.

The chairman held out his hand. "Thank you, Doctor."

Rigo shook it. His palm was dry. His grip was firm.

He walked down the marble hallway, past portraits of dead senators, their eyes following him. The elevator doors opened. He stepped in. Pressed the button for the ground floor.

The doors began to close.

Javier’s lawyer stepped forward, hand raised. The doors stopped.

She leaned in, voice low. "He told me to tell you—he doesn’t know Mateo is his son."

The doors shut.

Rigo stood in the elevator, staring at his reflection in the polished metal. His face was calm. His pulse was not.

The elevator descended.

Chapter 15

The clinic’s waiting room smelled of lemon wax and antiseptic. Sunlight cut through the blinds in pale stripes across the tiled floor. The door chimed. Rigo didn’t look up from the patient chart. Three more hours until his shift ended. Then he could walk the familiar streets of Palermo at dusk, past the same closed shops, the same cigarette smoke curling from doorways.

Inés Ibarra stepped inside. The blinds caught light on her dark glasses. She wore a tailored blouse in muted green, the kind of color that made her skin look warmer. A first-edition Borges glinted under her arm.

Rigo set down his pen.

She didn’t smile. “Hola, Doctor.”

“Señora Ibarra.” He gestured to the empty chair. “Still no appointment.”

She took the seat anyway. Laid the book on his desk. “For the doctor who saved us.”

Rigo didn’t touch it. “Fake dedication. I never saved anyone.”

“You kept the clinic open for free. That’s more than most doctors do.” She tapped the spine. “Borges belonged to my father.”

Rigo’s thumb twitched against the chart. “He didn’t mention you before.”

“He didn’t mention a lot of things.” She untucked her sunglasses. Her eyes were sharp, assessing. “I want you to see him.”

“Mateo?”

She nodded. “He’s in medical school now. Rotating through Hospital Italiano.”

Rigo exhaled through his nose. “He should pick a specialty that doesn’t put him in the same room as me.”

Inés leaned forward. “He wants to thank you.”

“He doesn’t.”

“He does.” She slid a sheet of paper across the desk. A photograph: Mateo in scrubs, stethoscope around his neck, younger than Rigo remembered. “He’s publishing soon. Wants your blessing.”

Rigo flipped the photo face-down. “No.”

“Why not?”

“Because I don’t give blessings. And I don’t give absolution.” He tapped the Borges. “Take it back. I don’t want it.”

Inés didn’t move. “You saved us from the clinic fees. The rent. The fear. That’s real. Even if you won’t admit it.”

Rigo stood. Closed the chart. “Thirty seconds to admire your own nobility. Then you leave.”

She smiled. Not a warm smile. A sharp one. “You’re afraid.”

He turned toward the sink. Ran water over his hands, scrubbed between the fingers. “I’m busy.”

“Mateo thinks you’re a coward.”

The water cut off. Rigo dried his hands slowly. “He’s not wrong.”

Inés’s voice dropped. “Then prove him wrong.”

Rigo looked at her. Really looked. The same stubborn set to her jaw, the same angles of her face, but older. Tougher. “You don’t get to decide what’s good for him.”

“Neither do you.” She stood. “He’s my brother.”

“And I failed him once. I won’t do it again.”

Inés picked up the Borges. Held it close to her chest. “He’s not the boy you remember.”

Rigo didn’t answer.

She set the book on his desk again. “Read the inscription inside. Then call him.”

The door chimed behind her.

Rigo waited five seconds. Then picked up the book. Flipped to the endpapers. The handwriting was cramped, precise:

*To the surgeon who saved us from more than just illness. — M.*

His breath caught.

The clinic’s phone rang. Shrill, insistent. He stared at the inscription. Then answered.

“Valente & Asociados.”

A woman’s voice, steady. “You know who this is.”

Rigo didn’t speak.

Cecilia said, “They’re coming for you.”

A pause. Not waiting for a reply.

“They know about the ledger. They know about Mateo. They think the game is over.” Her voice flattened. “But some ghosts don’t need a ledger to haunt the powerful.”

Rigo’s grip tightened on the phone. “When?”

“Now.” Static crackled. “They’re already outside.”

The line went dead.

Rigo hung up. Looked at the Borges. At the inscription. At the door.

Outside, tires screeched.

He moved fast. Shoved the book into a drawer. Locked the clinic from the inside. Crossed to the back exit, stepped into the alley’s cool shade.

A black Sedan idled at the curb. Two men in dark suits stood beside it. One held a file folder. The other a photograph.

The one with the folder spoke first. “Dr. Valente. Supreme Court orders your arrest for obstruction of justice and conspiracy.”

Rigo didn’t look at the photo. “You’re a day early.”

The man didn’t smile. “Get in the car.”

Rigo stepped back. “No.”

The other man raised the photograph. A crime scene shot: a body on wet pavement, a single gunshot to the temple. Mateo’s face.

Rigo’s pulse stuttered.

The first man’s eyes flicked to Rigo’s hands. “We know you made the call.”

Rigo exhaled. Short. Sharp.

Then he turned and ran.
